text.content:
"Count the moons. Name the days. When the last month turns and the sun does not rise—pray." — Skaldic saying

Overview

The people of Svellheim reckon time by a twelve-month calendar of thirty days each, yielding a year of 360 days. Years are counted from the founding of the first settlements and carry the postfix A.S.—After Settlement.

The calendar is lunar in spirit but solar in structure: the months are fixed at thirty days for administrative ease, while the moon (Máni) follows its own 29½-day cycle, drifting against the named months like a wheel within a wheel. Skalds, priests, and navigators track both; common folk count months and ignore the moon unless an omen demands otherwise.

The Months

The first six months belong to Sumar (Summer); the last six to Vetr (Winter). In living memory, these seasons meant something. Under Fimbulwinter, the names persist as tradition—a calendar remembering warmth that the world has forgotten.

# Month Season Associations
1 Harpa Sumar First-planting month. The season of broken ground and new seed—contracts signed, oaths renewed, apprenticeships started. The name means "to harp," suggesting song and celebration.
Under Fimbulwinter: The ground does not thaw, but Harpa is still observed as a time of renewal. People mend what they can and speak of what they will build when the cold breaks.
2 Skerpla Sumar The sharpening month. Named for the preparation of tools: ploughs, scythes, axes. The month of readiness—work is planned, resources tallied. Chainwardens conduct census counts.
Under Fimbulwinter: Skerpla is grim. The census reveals who died over the winter. The fuel tallies reveal how little remains.
3 Sólmánuður Sumar The Sun Month. The longest and brightest days—midsummer feasts, bonfires, and the great Alþing gatherings. The month of law, judgment, and open assembly. Disputes are tried, marriages made, debts settled under the high sun.
Under Fimbulwinter: The sun is a pale disc behind grey cloud. The Alþing still meets, but attendance dwindles. The bonfires burn lower each year.
4 Heyannir Sumar Hay-making month. The height of agricultural labour—cutting, drying, storing. Every hand works.
Under Fimbulwinter: There is no hay to make. The month is spent hunting, scavenging, and trading for what little dried food remains. Some settlements have begun calling Heyannir "Hunger-month."
5 Tvímánuður Sumar The Double Month. Day and night are roughly equal—the hinge-point of the year. The month of balance and reckoning; debts not settled at Alþing come due.
Under Fimbulwinter: The equal light reminds people that darkness is about to overtake it. Suicides increase. The Embertenders work hardest in Tvímánuður.
6 Haustmánuður Sumar Autumn Month. The last month of summer—the harvest is gathered and the year's success measured. Great feasts, the Vetrnætr festival at month's end, and the formal transition to Vetr.
Under Fimbulwinter: There is no harvest. Haustmánuður is the month of dread. Vetrnætr is still observed—offerings to the dead, who are no longer content to stay in their graves.
7 Gormánuður Vetr Slaughter Month. Livestock that cannot be fed through winter are slaughtered and preserved. The month of hard choices.
Under Fimbulwinter: There is little livestock left to slaughter. The choices are harder: which people can the settlement feed? Which must be turned away? The Chainwardens enforce rationing.
8 Ylir Vetr Yule Month. The great midwinter celebration: feasting, gift-giving, oaths, and the lighting of the Yule-fires. Traditionally the coldest month, redeemed by community.
Under Fimbulwinter: Yule is still observed, fiercely. The fires are smaller, but they burn. People cling to Ylir like a drowning man clings to driftwood.
9 Mörsugur Vetr Fat-Sucking Month. A bleak name for a bleak time: stored fat and preserved meat are consumed. The month of endurance.
Under Fimbulwinter: Mörsugur is the killing time. More people die in this month than any other. The cold is absolute. The dark is unrelenting.
10 Þorri Vetr Named for the frost-giant Þorri. The month of deepest cold and frozen earth. Observed with the Þorrablót—a ritual feast of preserved and fermented foods.
Under Fimbulwinter: The Þorrablót has taken on desperate urgency. Some communities have added blood-offerings. The Embertenders have formally forbidden this. It continues anyway.
11 Góa Vetr Named for Þorri's daughter. The month when winter should begin to loosen its grip—the first signs of thaw, the first birdsong, the first green shoot.
Under Fimbulwinter: Góa is the cruelest month. People watch for signs of thaw that never come. Some pretend—planting seeds in frozen ground, hanging birdhouses in dead trees. It is brave and heartbreaking.
12 Einmánuður Vetr The Lone Month. The last month of the year—standing alone between the worst of winter and the promise of spring. The month of vigil: waiting, watching, hoping.
Under Fimbulwinter: Einmánuður is when people ask, "Will this be the last year?" The transition to Harpa is observed with relief rather than joy. Another year survived. Another year of cold.

The Days of the Week

The Svellheim week has seven days. The first day is Sunnudagr, the Day of the Sun—a day of rest, reflection, and tending the Ever-Embers.

# Day Notes
1 Sunnudagr Sun-day. The day of rest. Embertenders lead observances. Markets are closed. Under Fimbulwinter, it is the day when communities gather to confirm the Ever-Ember still burns.
2 Mánadagr Moon-day. The workweek begins. Named for Máni, the moon—associated with industry and obligation.
3 Týsdagr Týr's day. Named for the god of law and single combat. A traditional day for legal proceedings, oaths, and duels. Chainwarden courts convene on Týsdagr.
4 Óðinsdagr Odin's day. Associated with wisdom, cunning, and sacrifice. Skalds and seers consider it auspicious for divination. Merchants consider it unlucky for contracts.
5 Þórsdagr Thor's day. Associated with strength, protection, and honest labour. The best day for building, forging, and physical work. Under Fimbulwinter, it is informally "repair day."
6 Frjádagr Freyja's day. Associated with love, beauty, and fertility. Traditional day for weddings and feasts. Under Fimbulwinter, it is the day when people try hardest to be human—to cook a good meal, tell a story, hold someone close.
7 Laugardagr Washing-day. The practical end of the week: bathing, cleaning, laundering, preparation for Sunnudagr. Even under Fimbulwinter, people maintain this routine. Cleanliness is one of the few dignities that costs nothing but effort.

The Seasons

Svellheim observes two seasons:

  • Sumar (Summer) — Months 1–6, Harpa through Haustmánuður. The season of light, growth, work, and law.
  • Vetr (Winter) — Months 7–12, Gormánuður through Einmánuður. The season of darkness, endurance, community, and survival.

The transition points are formally marked:

  • Sumarmál (First Day of Summer) — Harpa 1st. Celebrated with bonfires and the planting of the first seed (even if symbolic).
  • Vetrnætr (Winter Nights) — Haustmánuður 28th–30th. A three-day festival: feasting, ancestor-veneration, and the dousing and re-lighting of hearth-fires to symbolise renewal.

Under Fimbulwinter

The seasons no longer correspond to weather. Sumar months are cold. Vetr months are colder. Snow falls in Harpa. Ice forms in Heyannir. The distinction now marks degrees of desperation rather than changes of climate:

  • Sumar months: The cold is survivable. Travel is possible. Communities can trade and communicate. The Ever-Embers burn steadily.
  • Vetr months: The cold intensifies beyond natural endurance. Travel becomes lethal. Communities are isolated. The Ever-Embers burn lower, as if the darkness itself smothers them.

"In Sumar, you can live. In Vetr, you can only not die." — Old farmers' saying

The Moon: Máni

The moon of Svellheim is called Máni. Its cycle is 29½ days—slightly out of step with the 30-day months, so the moon drifts against the calendar by half a day each month. Over the course of a year, the moon falls approximately 6 days behind the month-count.

Lunar Phases

Phase Name Significance
New Moon Nýmáni Darkness. Ill-omened for travel but favourable for secrecy. Thieves, spies, and the desperate travel under Nýmáni.
Waxing Crescent Vaxandi Tungl Growth begins. Good for starting journeys, planting, and new ventures.
First Quarter Fyrsta Fjórðungur Half-light. A neutral time—neither lucky nor unlucky.
Waxing Gibbous Vaxandi Hálfmáni Approaching fullness. Restless-dead activity increases. Embertenders double their watches.
Full Moon Fullmáni The dead walk freely. Barrow-wights, draugr, and revenants are at their strongest. Communities bar their doors. The Chainwardens patrol in force. Under Fimbulwinter, the full moon is feared.
Waning Gibbous Þverrandi Hálfmáni The danger recedes. A good time for healing, cleansing, and repairing damage from the full moon.
Last Quarter Síðasta Fjórðungur Fading. A time for endings: settling debts, concluding trials, finishing work.
Waning Crescent Þverrandi Tungl The final sliver. A liminal time between cycles. Seers say the veil between worlds is thinnest here. Omens are read. Prophecies are spoken.

Moon and the Undead

Under Fimbulwinter, the moon's influence on the restless dead is measurable and terrifying. The pattern is well-documented by the Chainwardens:

Lunar Phase Undead Activity
New Moon (Nýmáni) Minimal. The dead are quiet.
Waxing Increases gradually. Scout patrols report movement in barrow-fields.
Full Moon (Fullmáni) Peak activity. Mass risings. Coordinated behaviour—the dead move in columns, as if directed. Sieges intensify. Communities that have lost their Ever-Embers are overrun.
Waning Decreases. The dead return to their graves—or simply stop moving.

⚠ Danger Compounds: A full moon in Mörsugur (month 9, deepest winter) is the most dangerous time in all of Svellheim. A new moon in Sólmánuður (month 3, midsummer) is the safest—and even then, "safe" means "fewer than usual."

Key Observances

Observance Date Description
Sumarmál Blót Harpa 1st Offerings for a good season. Bonfires, seed-planting, renewal of oaths.
Alþing Sólmánuður 15th–20th The great assembly. Law, judgment, trade, and politics. The most important civic event of the year. Suspended for three years running under Fimbulwinter.
Vetrnætr Haustmánuður 28th–30th Winter Nights. A three-day festival: ancestor-veneration, feasting, the transition to Vetr. Hearth-fires are doused and re-lit to symbolise renewal.
Yule Ylir 15th–21st Seven days of midwinter celebration. Gift-giving, oath-swearing, and the great Yule-fires.
Þorrablót Þorri 1st Feast of Þorri. Preserved foods, endurance rituals, and prayers for winter's end.

Things and Councils

  • Local Þing: Monthly at each settlement, typically the first Týsdagr of the month.
  • Regional Þing: Quarterly at major towns (Hrafnborg, Stairwatch, and others).
  • Alþing: Annual national assembly in Sólmánuður. Suspended under Fimbulwinter—travel too dangerous for outlying delegates.

The Current Date

The campaign begins on Sólmánuður, the 3rd day—early in the Sun Month, when the Alþing should be gathering and the world should be at its brightest and warmest.

Instead, the sun is a pale coin behind slate-grey cloud. Snow drifts against buildings that should be sun-warmed. The Ever-Ember at Hrafnborg burns, but lower than it did last Sólmánuður. And the one before that.

The Alþing will not meet this year. The roads are too dangerous. The delegates from the Interior Belt have not been heard from since Skerpla.
